docs.title: Introduction to Motion Capture Data Processing
docs.text:
Author: Arshad Naguru · Last Updated: March 2026 · Category: Tutorial

Learn how to work with motion capture (mocap) data — loading BVH/FBX files, visualizing joint data, and cleaning noisy captures.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Python 3.9+
  • Basic understanding of 3D coordinate systems

docs.title: Step 1: Install Required Libraries
docs.text: pip install numpy pandas matplotlib bvhio

docs.title: Step 2: Understanding Mocap File Formats
docs.text: Format Description Common Use BVH Biovision Hierarchy — text-based, widely supported Animation, research FBX Autodesk format — binary, supports meshes + animation Game dev, VFX C3D Biomechanics format — stores 3D marker positions Research, sports science CSV Raw marker positions exported from capture software Custom pipelines

docs.title: Step 3: Load and Inspect a BVH File
docs.text:
import numpy as np

def parse_bvh_basic(filepath):
    """Basic BVH parser to extract joint names and frame data."""
    with open(filepath, 'r') as f:
        lines = f.readlines()

    joints = []
    motion_start = 0

    for i, line in enumerate(lines):
        stripped = line.strip()
        if stripped.startswith("JOINT") or stripped.startswith("ROOT"):
            joint_name = stripped.split()[-1]
            joints.append(joint_name)
        if stripped == "MOTION":
            motion_start = i
            break

    # Parse frame count and frame time
    num_frames = int(lines[motion_start + 1].split(":")[1].strip())
    frame_time = float(lines[motion_start + 2].split(":")[1].strip())

    print(f"Joints: {len(joints)}")
    print(f"Frames: {num_frames}")
    print(f"Frame time: {frame_time}s ({1/frame_time:.0f} FPS)")

    return joints, num_frames, frame_time

joints, frames, ft = parse_bvh_basic("capture_data.bvh")

docs.title: Step 4: Clean Noisy Data
docs.text:
from scipy.signal import savgol_filter

def smooth_motion_data(data, window=11, poly_order=3):
    """Apply Savitzky-Golay filter to smooth noisy mocap data."""
    smoothed = np.copy(data)
    for joint_idx in range(data.shape[1]):
        for axis in range(3):  # x, y, z
            smoothed[:, joint_idx, axis] = savgol_filter(
                data[:, joint_idx, axis], window, poly_order
            )
    return smoothed

docs.title: Step 5: Visualize Joint Trajectories
docs.text:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def plot_joint_trajectory(data, joint_idx, joint_name):
    """Plot the 3D trajectory of a single joint over time."""
    fig, axes = plt.subplots(3, 1, figsize=(12, 8))
    labels = ['X', 'Y', 'Z']

    for i, ax in enumerate(axes):
        ax.plot(data[:, joint_idx, i], linewidth=0.8)
        ax.set_ylabel(f"{labels[i]} Position")
        ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

    axes[0].set_title(f"Joint Trajectory: {joint_name}")
    axes[2].set_xlabel("Frame")
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig(f"trajectory_{joint_name}.png", dpi=150)
    plt.show()

Warning

Always back up raw capture data before applying any cleaning or smoothing operations.

USE project_db;

    id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,

);

)

cap = cv2.VideoCapture(0)  # 0 = default webcam

docs.title: Image Classification with PyTorch
docs.text:
Author: Arshad Naguru · Last Updated: March 2026 · Category: Tutorial

Build an image classifier using PyTorch with transfer learning on a custom dataset.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Python 3.9+, PyTorch installed
  • GPU recommended for training

docs.title: Step 1: Install Dependencies
docs.text: pip install torch torchvision matplotlib pillow

docs.title: Step 2: Load and Prepare Data
docs.text:
import torch
from torchvision import datasets, transforms
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader

transform = transforms.Compose([
    transforms.Resize((224, 224)),
    transforms.RandomHorizontalFlip(),
    transforms.ToTensor(),
    transforms.Normalize(mean=[0.485, 0.456, 0.406], std=[0.229, 0.224, 0.225])
])

train_dataset = datasets.ImageFolder("data/train", transform=transform)
val_dataset = datasets.ImageFolder("data/val", transform=transform)

train_loader = DataLoader(train_dataset, batch_size=32, shuffle=True)
val_loader = DataLoader(val_dataset, batch_size=32, shuffle=False)

print(f"Classes: {train_dataset.classes}")
print(f"Training samples: {len(train_dataset)}")

docs.title: Step 3: Define the Model
docs.text:
from torchvision import models
import torch.nn as nn

model = models.resnet50(pretrained=True)

# Freeze all layers
for param in model.parameters():
    param.requires_grad = False

# Replace final layer for our number of classes
num_classes = len(train_dataset.classes)
model.fc = nn.Linear(model.fc.in_features, num_classes)

device = torch.device("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")
model = model.to(device)

docs.title: Step 4: Train
docs.text:
criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()
optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.fc.parameters(), lr=0.001)

for epoch in range(10):
    model.train()
    running_loss = 0.0
    correct = 0
    total = 0

    for images, labels in train_loader:
        images, labels = images.to(device), labels.to(device)

        optimizer.zero_grad()
        outputs = model(images)
        loss = criterion(outputs, labels)
        loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()

        running_loss += loss.item()
        _, predicted = outputs.max(1)
        total += labels.size(0)
        correct += predicted.eq(labels).sum().item()

    accuracy = 100.0 * correct / total
    print(f"Epoch {epoch+1}: Loss={running_loss/len(train_loader):.4f}, Acc={accuracy:.2f}%")

docs.title: Step 5: Save the Model
docs.text:
torch.save(model.state_dict(), "model_weights.pth")
print("Model saved successfully")

docs.title: Building a Data Dashboard with Streamlit
docs.text:
Author: Arshad Naguru · Last Updated: March 2026 · Category: Tutorial

Build an interactive data dashboard using Streamlit — load data, create charts, and add user controls.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:

  • Familiarity with Pandas and Matplotlib

docs.title: Step 1: Install Streamlit
docs.text: pip install streamlit pandas plotly

docs.title: Step 2: Create the Dashboard
docs.text:
Create dashboard.py:

import streamlit as st
import pandas as pd
import plotly.express as px

st.set_page_config(page_title="Analytics Dashboard", layout="wide")
st.title("Project Analytics Dashboard")

# Sidebar controls
st.sidebar.header("Filters")
date_range = st.sidebar.date_input("Date Range", value=[])
category = st.sidebar.selectbox("Category", ["All", "Tutorial", "How-To", "Reference"])

# Load data
@st.cache_data
def load_data():
    return pd.read_csv("data.csv")

df = load_data()

# Metrics row
col1, col2, col3 = st.columns(3)
col1.metric("Total Documents", len(df))
col2.metric("Contributors", df["author"].nunique())
col3.metric("This Month", len(df[df["created_at"] >= "2026-03-01"]))

# Chart
fig = px.bar(df.groupby("category").size().reset_index(name="count"),
             x="category", y="count", title="Documents by Category")
st.plotly_chart(fig, use_container_width=True)

# Data table
st.subheader("Recent Documents")
st.dataframe(df.sort_values("created_at", ascending=False).head(20))

docs.title: Getting Started with Unity for VR
docs.text:
Author: Arshad Naguru · Last Updated: March 2026 · Category: Tutorial

Create your first interactive VR scene in Unity with hand tracking and object interaction.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Unity 2022 LTS with XR packages installed (see VR Dev Environment Setup)
  • VR headset connected

docs.title: Step 1: Create a New VR Project
docs.text:
1. Open Unity Hub → New Project
  2. Select 3D (URP) template
  3. Name it and click Create

docs.title: Step 2: Set Up the XR Rig
docs.text:
1. Delete the default Main Camera
  2. Right-click in Hierarchy → XR → XR Origin (VR)
  3. This creates a camera rig that tracks your headset and controllers

docs.title: Step 3: Add Interactable Objects
docs.text:
using UnityEngine;
using UnityEngine.XR.Interaction.Toolkit;

public class GrabbableObject : MonoBehaviour
{
    private XRGrabInteractable grabInteractable;

    void Start()
    {
        grabInteractable = GetComponent<XRGrabInteractable>();
        grabInteractable.selectEntered.AddListener(OnGrab);
        grabInteractable.selectExited.AddListener(OnRelease);
    }

    private void OnGrab(SelectEnterEventArgs args)
    {
        Debug.Log($"Grabbed: {gameObject.name}");
    }

    private void OnRelease(SelectExitEventArgs args)
    {
        Debug.Log($"Released: {gameObject.name}");
    }
}

docs.title: Step 4: Add a 3D Environment
docs.text:
1. Right-click in Hierarchy → 3D Object → Plane (floor)
  2. Add cubes and spheres as interactable objects
  3. Add Rigidbody and XR Grab Interactable components to each object

docs.title: Step 5: Build and Test
docs.text:
1. File → Build Settings → Select your platform
  2. Click Build and Run
  3. Pick up and throw objects in VR

docs.title: Setting Up a VR Development Environment
docs.text:
Author: Arshad Naguru · Last Updated: March 2026 · Category: Tutorial

This tutorial walks you through configuring a development environment for VR/XR projects, covering headset setup, SDK installation, and your first test scene.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• A VR headset (Meta Quest 2/3 or HTC Vive recommended)
  • PC with a dedicated GPU (NVIDIA GTX 1060 or higher)
  • Unity Hub installed

docs.title: Step 1: Install Unity with VR Support
docs.text:
1. Download and install Unity Hub
  2. Install Unity 2022 LTS or newer
  3. During installation, check Android Build Support (for Quest) or Windows Build Support
# Verify Unity installation via CLI (optional)
unity -version

docs.title: Step 2: Install the XR Plugin
docs.text:
Open your Unity project and install the required XR packages:

  1. Go to Window → Package Manager
  2. Search for and install:
  3. XR Interaction Toolkit
  4. OpenXR Plugin
  5. XR Plugin Management

docs.title: Step 3: Configure XR Settings
docs.text:
1. Go to Edit → Project Settings → XR Plug-in Management
  2. Enable OpenXR for your target platform
  3. Under OpenXR settings, add your headset's interaction profile

docs.title: Step 4: Create a Test Scene
docs.text:
// Simple XR Origin setup script
using UnityEngine;
using UnityEngine.XR.Interaction.Toolkit;

public class VRTestSetup : MonoBehaviour
{
    void Start()
    {
        Debug.Log("VR Environment Initialized Successfully");
    }
}

docs.title: Step 5: Build and Test
docs.text:
1. Connect your headset via USB (or set up wireless debugging)
  2. Go to File → Build Settings → Build and Run
  3. Put on your headset — you should see the test scene

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Issue Solution Headset not detected Check USB connection and install headset drivers Black screen in headset Verify OpenXR is enabled in Project Settings Low frame rate Reduce scene complexity or check GPU drivers
